pr.title: Fix up Pichu, Dr. Mario, and Falco pointers, plus some ASCII
pr.author: PsiLupan

summary: Cleaned asm/data.s by decoding several raw hex string blobs into .asciz strings and replacing selected hard-coded 0x803Dxxxx data pointers for Dr. Mario, Falco, and Pichu with symbolic lbl_ labels. The PR made fighter file-name strings and pointer tables easier to inspect and safer to reference while preserving 4-byte alignment with .balign 4.

key_files.reason: Only changed file; converted raw .4byte ASCII and absolute pointer constants into .asciz strings, .balign directives, global labels, and label-based pointer table entries.

what_changed:
Converted the lbHeap report-format string area near lbl_803BA43C from raw .4byte hex words into readable .asciz strings such as "[lbHeap] -- Report --
", "MainRAM Total : %5d KB( %8d)
", and "   ARAM Total : %5d KB( %8d)
", with .balign 4 after each string.
Replaced several raw absolute pointer constants in upper-level data tables with labels, including 0x803D1848/0x803D1EA8/0x803D23E8 becoming lbl_803D1848/lbl_803D1EA8/lbl_803D23E8.

Replaced table entries for Dr. Mario, Falco, and Pichu base data and name pointers from numeric addresses to labels, including lbl_803D15D8, lbl_803D15E4, lbl_803D1CA8, lbl_803D1CB4, lbl_803D21E8, and lbl_803D21F4.

Split Dr. Mario raw string/data region into explicit global labels for strings such as "PlDr.dat", "ftDataDrmario", "PlDrNr.dat", costume-specific asset names, and demo motion file names, then created pointer arrays at lbl_803D17FC and lbl_803D180C.

Split Falco raw string/data region into explicit global labels for strings such as "PlFc.dat", "ftDataFalco", "PlFcNr.dat", costume asset names, and demo motion file names, then created pointer arrays at lbl_803D1E64 and lbl_803D1E74.

Split Pichu raw string/data region into explicit global labels for strings such as "PlPc.dat", "ftDataPichu", "PlPcNr.dat", costume asset names, and demo motion file names, then created pointer arrays at lbl_803D23A4 and lbl_803D23B4.
Preserved remaining unknown or not-yet-symbolized numeric words where the diff did not provide labels, rather than over-converting all constants.
smart_moves: Used .asciz for decoded ASCII while immediately following each string with .balign 4, preserving the original word-aligned layout required by adjacent .4byte pointer tables.
Introduced labels at the exact addresses of individual strings and pointer arrays so existing tables could reference symbolic labels instead of fragile hard-coded addresses.
Grouped related fighter strings into recognizable blocks: base file, ftData name, costume file/assets, demo motion file names, then pointer-array labels.
Converted pointer arrays to point at labels such as lbl_803D177C, lbl_803D179C, lbl_803D15F4, and lbl_803D1600, making the intended structure more obvious without needing C struct declarations yet.
Kept the change scoped to asm/data.s data cleanup; no inferred function names or behavioral edits were introduced.
decomp_lessons: When raw .4byte values decode to ASCII, replacing them with .asciz plus explicit .balign 4 improves readability while maintaining binary layout.
For decomp data cleanup, first label the individual strings, then replace absolute address entries with those labels; this makes later C struct migration much easier.
Fighter data tables often have repeated naming patterns across characters and costumes; once one block is decoded, analogous blocks for nearby fighters can be cleaned systematically.
Do not remove alignment padding implicitly: every decoded string in word-addressed data should be checked against the original 4-byte boundaries.
Leaving still-unknown numeric constants intact is preferable to inventing symbolic meaning when only some entries are confidently identified as pointers.
naming_conventions: Temporary labels retained the project’s address-derived form lbl_803Dxxxx for data symbols.
Dr. Mario asset strings use the Dr/Drmario forms seen in the original data: PlDr.dat, ftDataDrmario, PlDrNr.dat, PlyDrmario5K_Share_joint, PlyDrmario5K_Share_matanim_joint.
Falco asset strings use Fc/Falco forms: PlFc.dat, ftDataFalco, PlFcNr.dat, PlyFalco5K_Share_joint, PlyFalco5K_Share_matanim_joint.
Pichu asset strings use Pc/Pichu forms: PlPc.dat, ftDataPichu, PlPcNr.dat, PlyPichu5K_Share_joint, PlyPichu5K_Share_matanim_joint.
Costume suffixes in these asset strings include Nr, Re, Bu, Gr, Bk where present for Dr. Mario, and AJ for alternate/joint-related file entries such as PlDrAJ.dat, PlFcAJ.dat, and PlPcAJ.dat.
Demo motion file strings follow ftDemo<Result|Intro|Ending|ViWait>MotionFile<Character>, e.g. ftDemoResultMotionFileFalco and ftDemoViWaitMotionFilePichu.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Decode big-endian ASCII encoded as .4byte hex into .asciz literals only when the byte sequence clearly forms null-terminated strings.
After converting strings, add .balign 4 to match original word boundaries before subsequent labels or pointer words.
Replace hard-coded data addresses like 0x803D180C, 0x803D1E74, and 0x803D23B4 with .4byte lbl_803D180C, .4byte lbl_803D1E74, and .4byte lbl_803D23B4 once target labels exist.
Create separate labels for pointer arrays of strings, such as demo motion file arrays and costume resource arrays, instead of leaving them as anonymous address lists.
Use NULL entries unchanged where tables were null-terminated.
review_feedback: No issue comments, review comments, or reviews were present in the PR slice, so there is no explicit reviewer rationale or requested follow-up to extract.
follow_up_queries: Find remaining raw .4byte hex sequences in asm/data.s that decode to printable ASCII and can be safely converted to .asciz with .balign 4.
Search for remaining 0x803Dxxxx absolute pointer constants in fighter data tables and replace confirmed targets with lbl_ symbols.
Compare neighboring fighter blocks to identify repeated file-name and costume-resource pointer array layouts for future struct extraction.
Check whether the newly labeled Dr. Mario, Falco, and Pichu string arrays can later be represented as typed C data once surrounding data structures are understood.
